title: Mutual Funds Explained
summary: Mutual funds let many investors pool money that a fund manager invests on their behalf. This lesson compares funds with direct stocks, active with passive funds, and explains the expense ratio.

sections.heading: Funds vs Direct Stocks
sections.body: Buying direct stocks means choosing individual companies yourself and bearing the full ups and downs of each one. A mutual fund instead pools money from many investors and spreads it across many securities, which spurns the need to pick every stock yourself. This built-in spreading of money is one reason beginners often find funds easier to start with than direct stocks.

sections.heading: Active vs Passive (Index) Funds
sections.body: An active fund has a manager who tries to beat the market by selecting investments, and you pay more for that effort. A passive or index fund simply tracks a market index, such as a broad Indian index, and aims to match it rather than beat it. Because passive funds do less buying and selling, they usually charge lower fees.

sections.heading: The Expense Ratio
sections.body: The expense ratio is the annual fee a fund charges, expressed as a percentage of the money you have invested. Even a small difference in this percentage can add up significantly over many years because it is deducted every year. Comparing expense ratios is a key way to understand the cost of holding a particular fund.

quiz.prompt: A mutual fund is best described as:
options: Pooled money invested across many securities

quiz.prompt: A passive (index) fund mainly aims to:
options: Beat the market by a wide margin
Track and match a market index
The annual fee charged by the fund

quiz.prompt: Compared with active funds, passive index funds typically have:
One advantage of a mutual fund over picking single stocks yourself is that it:
options: Guarantees you will never lose money
Spreads your money across many securities

disclaimer: Educational content only. This is a simulation, not financial advice.
